coredata.dc:title: High peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and its application in immunosorbent assay
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Weiqiang
Huang, TingTing
Zhao, Mengmeng
Luo, Fang
Weng, Wen
Wei, Qiaohua
Lin, Zhenyu
Chen, Guonan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe-N-CDs) were synthesized through a solvothermal method assisting with the microwave synthesis system for the first time. Multiple techniques were employed to characterize the properties of the obtained particles. The emphasis of current work was the confirmation of their intrinsic peroxidase activity due to the Fe doping, which is similar to natural ferriporphyrin. Through catalyzing the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2, the Fe-N-CDs exhibited a superior catalytic performance over horseradish peroxidase (HRP), suggesting their potential applications in catalytic area. Furthermore, the Fe-N-CDs were coupled to antibody as the biocatalyst instead of HRP (commonly used in enzyme-linked immunosorbnent assay (ELISA)). Results of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) determination demonstrated the feasibility of this approach, a detection limit as low as 0.1pg/mL within 5min has been achieved. These advances may trigger research interest in Fe-N-CDs and their new applications in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron
Carbon dots
High peroxidase-like activity
Biocatalyst
Immunosorbent assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the peroxidase mimetic Fe-N-CDs
Analytical procedures for the Fe-N-CDs peroxidase activity
Application of Fe-N-CDs in immunosorbnent assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the synthetic Fe-N-CDs. Inset: size distribution. (B) FTIR spectrum of Fe-N-CDs. (C) XPS analysis of Fe-N-CDs in full spectrum. (D) XPS spectra in the Fe 2p region of Fe-N-CDs.

(A) UV–vis spectra of TMB (a), TMB/H2O2 (b), TMB/N-CDs/H2O2 (c) and TMB/Fe-N-CDs/H2O2 (d) in NaAc buffer after incubation of 2min. Inset: corresponding photographs. (B) Time-dependent absorbance variation of TMB/H2O2 solution at 652nm analyzed by Fe-N-CDs in different amounts: (a) 1, (b) 2, (c) 5, (d) 10, (e) 20, (f) 50μg. Insert: Typical absorbance spectra varied with time. Experiments were carried out with in NaAc buffer solution with 100µm TMB and 10µmH2O2.

Influences of pH (A) and temperature (B) on the catalytic performance of Fe-N-CDs and HRP. The maximum point in each curve was set as 100%. (C) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm of TMB/H2O2 solution analyzed by HRP, Fe-N-CDs, and Fe-N-CDs stored after two months. (D) Absorbance changes at 652nm of the Fe-N-CDs mediated catalytic oxidization of TMB by H2O2 ranging from 0.1 to 100µm. Inset: Linear relationship between the absorbance and the logarithmic concentration of H2O2 with corresponding photographs. Data are presented as average±sd from three independent measurements. Experiments were carried out using 10μg/mL Fe-N-CDs or 250U/mg HRP as catalyst with 100µm TMB and 10µmH2O2 in buffer solution.

(A) Schematic illustration of the Fe-N-CDs employed immunosorbnent assay. (B) Comparison of the CEA detection based on immunosorbnent assay employing Fe-N-CDs (red plots) and HRP (blue plots) as biocatalyst respectively. Operations followed the ELISA method and the reagents offered by the CEA ELISA Kit. Data are presented as average±sd from three independent measurements.
Illustration of the formation progress of Fe-N-CDs and its peroxidase mimetic activity.
Comparison of this work with previous studies regarding the performance of H2O2 detection using Fe-N-CDs, carbon-based materials, and Fe-based nanoparticles.
Determination of CEA in human blood serum employing Fe-N-CDs as biocatalysts.

head.ce:title: High peroxidase-like activity of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots and its application in immunosorbent assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe-N-CDs) were synthesized through a solvothermal method assisting with the microwave synthesis system for the first time. Multiple techniques were employed to characterize the properties of the obtained particles. The emphasis of current work was the confirmation of their intrinsic peroxidase activity due to the Fe doping, which is similar to natural ferriporphyrin. Through catalyzing the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2, the Fe-N-CDs exhibited a superior catalytic performance over horseradish peroxidase (HRP), suggesting their potential applications in catalytic area. Furthermore, the Fe-N-CDs were coupled to antibody as the biocatalyst instead of HRP (commonly used in enzyme-linked immunosorbnent assay (ELISA)). Results of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) determination demonstrated the feasibility of this approach, a detection limit as low as 0.1pg/mL within 5min has been achieved. These advances may trigger research interest in Fe-N-CDs and their new applications in various fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial enzymes have received considerable attention due to their advantages over natural enzymes, such as controlled synthesis in low cost, tunability in catalytic activities, and high stability against stringent conditions [1]. Many nanomaterials have exhibited unexpected enzyme-like activity, and great advances have been achieved in this area due to the unique characteristics of nanomaterials. Such nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes (nanozymes) have already found wide applications in biosensor, cancer therapy, energy transfer, and pollutant removal [2,3]. Among these nanozymes, great interest has been focused on the peroxidase-like nanozymes because it closely associates with oxygen-reduction reaction (ORR) and widely involves in many fields. Nanomaterial like metal nanoparticles [4–7], carbon nanomaterials [8–10], and other nanocomposites [11–13] possess certain peroxidase-like activity. However, the current catalyst materials are still far from meeting the combined requirements of high catalytic activity, strong durability, and low cost in practical applications. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a robust nanomaterial as an effective peroxidase mimetic.

Recent research reveals that nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) [14,15] fully complement the defect of carbon dots (CDs) in single energy level and thus arouse a wider applications in photocatalysis [16], biosensor [17], energy-storage [18], and pollutant degradation [19]. Nevertheless, extensive efforts have been devoted on doping CDs with heteroatom (i.e., B, S, P, F, and Ge) to exploit unique properties lately [20–23]. Additionally, metal elements were also regarded as another category of potential candidates for CDs doping [24–26]. A hydrothermal synthetic approach was reported to prepare copper-containing CDs which exhibited laccase-like activities responding to p-phenylenediamine [24]. Na2[Cu(EDTA)] was recently employed as precursor to prepared Cu–N-doped CDs [27], research showed that the photocatalytic efficiency was greatly improved as a result of the Cu–N doping. Compared with copper, iron mediated catalytic ORR was even more widespread, not only existing in the ferriporphyrin containing protein [28] but also in many iron carbide nanoparticles [29–33]. And the facts have proved that their catalytic capability mainly stems from the coordination of iron with nitrogen doping in carbon. However, complicated preparation procedures hinder their large-scale synthesis, and the barely modification sites also limit their further wide applications [29–31]. Fortunately, N-CDs provide us an alternative material as the framework, which are usually synthesized by a facile pyrolysis of raw material containing carbon and nitrogen. By doping iron additionally, it holds a great promising to acquire superior catalytic property. It thus encourages us to develop a facile method to prepare iron-doped CDs with outstanding catalytic performance, and focus on their practical applications.

In this work, iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe-N-CDs) were synthesized by a one-pot, rapid, and convenient solvothermal method assisting with the microwave synthesis system. The obtained Fe-N-CDs exhibit a homogeneous diameter in spherical shape with an excellent water-dispersibility. Because the synthetic particles are doped with iron, they are demonstrated to possess intrinsic peroxidase activity through catalyzing the oxidation of 3, 3, 5, 5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a deeper color in the presence of H2O2 ( Scheme 1). Compared with natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the prepared Fe-N-CDs show a stable performance of catalysis either at a wider pH range or under varying temperature conditions, vastly expanding their practical applications. Accordingly, a colorimetric method for optical H2O2 detection was developed by using Fe-N-CDs as catalyst. Furthermore, the Fe-N-CDs were employed as the biocatalyst instead of HRP by chemically coupling to antibody to develop a colorimetric method for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) determination based on immunosorbent assay method [34]. The result suggests their bright prospects in biosensor and medical diagnostics.

L-Tartaric acid (TA), urea, FeCl3·6H2O, oleic acid, and H2O2 (30wt%) were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). TMB and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)−3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and dissolved immediately before used. Human CEA ELISA Kit, HRP, Alpha fetoprotein (AFP), bovine serum albumin (BSA), immunoglobulin G (IgG), and thrombin (TB) were purchased from BOSTER Biotech Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China). All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ.cm) was prepared with a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout all experiments.

The Fe-N-CDs were synthesized by a single-mode microwave synthesis system (Monowave 300, Anton Paar GmbH). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were performed on Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI Company, USA), with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was recorded using a Thermo Escalab 250 Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) with Al/Kα as the X-ray source for excitation. The iron content was measured by atomic absorption spectroscopy (PerkinElmer PinAAcle 900, USA). UV–vis measurements were carried out on a Specord ®200 plus spectrophotometer (Analytikjena, Germany) and the slit width was 1nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Magna-IR 750 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay was carried out on a microplate spectrophotometer (Tecan Group Ltd., Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the peroxidase mimetic Fe-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe-N-CDs were synthesized by a one-step microwave synthesis method. Typically, 0.2g of TA, 1.0g of urea, and 0.2g of FeCl3·6H2O were firstly ground well and transferred into a microwave tube filled with 10mL of oleic acid as the reaction solvent. The solution was heated to 210℃ within 1min by microwave and maintained for 10min, then cooled down to room temperature. Equal volume of ultrapure water and n-hexane were added to the mixed solution, followed by repeatedly extractions to remove the unreacted oleic acid. The aqueous solution of Fe-N-CDs was purified via silica column chromatography using a mixture of ethyl acetate and ethanol (1:19 in volume ratio) as the eluent. After removing solvents by rotary evaporation and further drying under vacuum, the precipitates were weighed and re-dispersed in water at a concentration of 1mg/mL. As control, reagents without FeCl3·6H2O were used for synthesis of Fe-free N-CDs on the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedures for the Fe-N-CDs peroxidase activity
ce:section.ce:para: TMB was freshly prepared in ethanol with a concentration of 1mM. Then TMB (100μL) and various concentrations of H2O2 (100μL) solution were added to NaAc buffer solution (50mM, pH 4.0), followed by adding 100μL of diluted Fe-N-CDs (or N-CDs in control) to the mixture to a volume of 1.0mL. The mixture solution was blended by Vortex and the absorbance was measured at 652nm [13].

Conjugation of Fe-N-CDs to antibody was performed through the EDC-catalyzed reaction between carboxyl and amine groups. Briefly, 1mL of antibody was completely mixed with 10mL of carboxyl-containing Fe-N-CDs solution (1mg/mL) in PBS buffer (10mM, pH 7.4). Then, 200μL of freshly-prepared EDC solution (5mg/mL) was added to the solution, and the above mixture was magnetically stirred for 4h at 37℃. Finally, the particles were centrifuged (8000g) and washed with PBS three times. The resulting Fe-N-CDs labeled antibody were re-dispersed in PBS and stored at 4°C for later use.

In a typical determination, 100μL of various concentrations of CEA-containing samples were added to the capture antibody-coated wells and incubated on a shaker for 30min. After draining the samples and washing repeatedly, 100μL of Fe-N-CDs labeled antibody (10μg/mL) were added into the wells. The plate was incubated at 37℃ for another 30min, then drained and washed three times by wash buffer. 100μL of TMB-containing solution was then added for the development of color at different degrees. The catalytic reaction was terminated by H2SO4 after 5min, and the result was analyzed by a microplate spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1A shows the typical TEM image of the as-prepared Fe-N-CDs by microwave synthesis system. The Fe-N-CDs were nearly spherical and distributed uniformly, the maximum population was estimated to be 2.8nm. From the image of HRTEM (Fig. S1), the lattice fringes of Fe-N-CDs were observed and calculated to be 0.22nm, which corresponds to the [100] facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [35]. This evidently confirms the formation of carbon based framework of the resultant particles. Fig. 1B presents the FTIR spectrum of the obtained particles. The peak around 1400cm−1 was assigned to aromatic C˭C and C˭N stretching vibrations, agreeing with the framework of carbon nitride [36]. And the intense band around 1700 and 3200cm−1 were attributed to asymmetric C˭O and O-H stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of carboxyl group. It is crucial for a further modification of these particles and also endows them an excellent water-dispersibility.

XPS was employed to analyze the composition and surface state of the obtained particles. Fig. 1C reveals the existence of three main elements C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. Particularly, the C 1s XPS spectrum was resolved into five peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 286.2, 288.1, and 288.7eV (Fig. S2A), corresponding to five types of carbon bonds: C˭C (sp2), C-C (sp3), C–N or C–O, N–C˭N (sp2), and C˭O, respectively [37]. Also the N 1s spectrum for Fe–N–CDs contained four peaks at 398.5, 399.3, 400.6, and 402.8eV, which were assigned to pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen, and pyridinic N+-O-, respectively (Fig. S2B) [38]. These results demonstrate nitrogen had reacted with carbon and successfully doped in the particles. Besides, the peak around 710eV in XPS spectrum also reveals the existence of iron element in the synthetic particles. As shown in Fig. 1D, the Fe 2p XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into four types of Fe species, corresponding to Fe2+2p3/2 (710.5eV), Fe3+2p3/2 (713.4eV), Fe2+2p1/2 (723.2eV), Fe3+2p1/2 (725.9eV), respectively [39]. Note that pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms can coordinate with Fe to form Fe-Nx active site [40] in metal nitrides/carbon materials [26], therefore, it is considered as the active site of catalysis in this work. To obtain a maximum doping ratio of iron, different dosage of FeCl3·6H2O was investigated. As the atomic absorption spectroscopy revealed (Fig. S3), when the dosage of FeCl3·6H2O surpassed 0.2g, the iron content in the Fe-N-CDs did not increase further and the doping ratio reached up to a maximum of 1.6%.

Further studies focusing on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-N-CDs were carried out by catalyzing the oxidation of TMB to a deeper color in presence of H2O2. Fig. 2A shows the absorption spectra and corresponding photographs of TMB, TMB/H2O2, TMB/CDs/H2O2, and TMB/Fe-N-CDs/H2O2 in NaAc buffer after 2min mixing. Almost no change in color was observed in TMB, TMB/H2O2, and TMB/N-CDs/H2O2 solutions. But a visible blue color change appeared in the TMB/Fe-N-CDs/H2O2 solution. These results indicate that Fe-N-CDs indeed have greatly peroxidase mimetic activity to catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2. Given that the catalytic reaction can be detected by monitoring TMB absorbance change at 652nm, the corresponding UV–vis spectra were collected to clearly reveal the differences. As shown in Fig. 2A, weak absorption peaks at 652nm from TMB/H2O2 and TMB/N-CDs/H2O2 solution were observed and the intensity of these two peaks were nearly equal, which indicates N-CDs without active iron barely have peroxidase activity. If N-CDs were replaced with Fe-N-CDs, a significant absorption peak was detected; its intensity was many times higher than that of formers. Additionally, the catalytic capability of Fe-N-CDs was also investigated by recording the time-dependent variation of TMB/H2O2 absorbance. The kinetic curves in Fig. 2B suggest that catalytic performance of Fe-N-CDs enhanced with amount increasing as well as time extending. These characters are the same with those of the nature enzyme. Catalytic performance of the system can be easily controlled in suitable range by adjusting the amount of Fe-N-CDs in practical applications. Inset in Fig. 2B also displays a typical absorbance spectra varied with time.

The catalytic performance of Fe-N-CDs was compared with natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) to gain a further insight into the peroxidase mimetic behaviors. Factors including pH and temperature that dramatically affect the catalytic performances of natural peroxidase were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 3A, the effect of pH on the oxidization of TMB by H2O2 had the same trend when it catalyzed by HRP or Fe-N-CDs. But Fe-N-CDs obviously preserved a comparable catalytic activity whether the pH downed to 1 or rose up to 12, which would lead to a fast inactivation of HRP. As regard to the effect of temperature, Fe-N-CDs also exhibited a superior catalytic stability over HRP. When the temperature varied from 20 to 60℃, the activity of Fe-N-CDs maintained over 71% while the activity of HRP downed under 10% (Fig. 3B). Under optimal conditions, the time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm were recorded using different concentrations of Fe-N-CDs and HRP. Fig. 3C shows the activity of Fe-N-CDs (100μL, 10μg/mL) was similar to that of natural HRP (0.5 U). So the activity of Fe-N-CDs was defined as 500U/mg. After storage at room temperature for two months, the activity of Fe-N-CDs hardly decreased, indicating the stability of the proposed material. Consequently, the as-prepared Fe-N-CDs offer a good platform for the effectively catalytic oxidization of TMB by H2O2 even in unfavourable circumstance. Based on the superior peroxidase property of Fe-N-CDs, a better catalytic performance achieved in many HRP involved reactions. For instance, the concentration of H2O2 can be visually determined through the color variation of TMB oxidation catalyzed by Fe-N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3D, the absorbance at 652nm increased proportional to the H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.1–100µm. The detection limit was estimated to be 0.05µm, which is far lower than traditionally colorimetric method.

Table 1 lists the comparison of the applications of Fe-N-CDs, carbon-based materials, and Fe-based nanocomposites in H2O2 detection. Because Fe-N-CDs possess catalytic active sites of Fe-N, it exhibited a superior performance in speed and sensitivity over previously reported peroxidase mimetic. The improvement of nanozymes is meaningful in hydrogen peroxide involved areas. For instance, the assay of some biomolecules, such as glucose, uric acid, cholesterol and so on [10,41,42], heavily relies on the product of hydrogen peroxide, and a superior nanozyme used would lead to a better result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Fe-N-CDs in immunosorbnent assay
ce:section.ce:para: Classical ELISA, which employs protein enzymes as biocatalysts to afford amplified signals, often suffers from low stability caused by the irreversible denaturation of these enzymes under harsh conditions, such as heat and acidity [43]. Considering the superior catalytic property of Fe-N-CDs and their abundant carboxy groups, an important application of Fe-N-CDs is expected to couple antibody as the biocatalyst, taking the place of HRP in immunosorbent assay. In this study, an immunosorbent assay of CEA employing Fe-N-CDs as the biocatalyst was developed ( Fig. 4A). The Fe-N-CDs labeled antibody was prepared by the EDC-catalyzed condensation reaction. Then it combined with the present antigen and capture antibody to form a sandwich complex, catalyzing the oxidization of TMB solution to form a deeper color. The concentration of CEA was quantitatively detected by the color degrees. Fig. 4B shows the relationship between the absorbance at 450nm (terminated by H2SO4) and the concentrations of CEA from 1 to 105 pg/mL, employing Fe-N-CDs (red points) or HRP (blue points) as biocatalysts, respectively. Apparently, immunosorbent assay employing Fe-N-CDs exhibited a better sensitivity. Notably, the naked-eye readout of CEA by immunosorbent assay employing Fe-N-CDs was as low as 100pg/mL, which was only 1% of that obtained from classical ELISA method. The recorded absorbance had a linear relationship with CEA concentration ranging from 1 to 1000pg/mL with a detection limit of 0.1pg/mL.

The specificity of the proposed immunosorbent assay employing Fe-N-CDs as biocatalyst was also evaluated by the detection of some analogous proteins, including AFP, human IgG, TB, and BSA, under identical experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. S4, the relative absorbance intensity change caused by CEA could be clearly distinguished from other biomolecules, which illustrates pronounced specificity of the method towards CEA. The reason lies in the highly specific recognition between CEA and its antibodies.

The reliability and practical applicability of the Fe-N-CDs involved immunosorbent assay was evaluated by the determination of the recovery of spiked CEA in human serum samples. The serum sample was firstly diluted 10-folds with PBS (pH 7.0, 0.1M), followed by the addition of different concentrations of CEA standard solutions. Results in Table 2 show that the recoveries are in the ranged of 96.6–106% and the RSD was in the range of 2.2–3.5%. So, the proposed immunosorbent assay employing Fe-N-CDs as biocatalysts demonstrates acceptable and desired feasibility for CEA detection, which might be a potential and promising method for the disease diagnosis related to CEA in clinical medicine.

In summary, Fe-N-CDs were first synthesized by a facile solvothermal method. The prepared Fe-N-CDs show not only well dimensional homogeneity and excellent water-dispersibility, but also the intrinsic peroxidase activity due to the doping of Fe. In comparison with HRP, Fe-N-CDs exhibit outstanding performance in catalytic oxidization of TMB to a deeper color in the presence of H2O2, under a wider range of pH and temperature conditions. Accordingly, a colorimetric method for optical H2O2 detection has been developed using the Fe-N-CDs as catalyst. Based on this preliminarily work, Fe-N-CDs labeled antibody has been successfully employed in determination of CEA based on immunosorbent assay. Results demonstrate that it is a promising probe in a lot of chemical and biological detection. The great contribution of this work lies in the construction of peroxidase mimetic Fe-N-CDs through a facile approach with superior catalytic performance, enabling this nanomaterial have great potential applications in numerous fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was partly financially supported by National Sciences Foundation of China (21275031, 21575027), program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-12–0619), the Natural Sciences Funding of Fujian Province (2014J01046, 2014J06005) and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT15R11).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.10.099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
